question_text: Mango (Mangifera indica) is often called the 'King of Fruits' and India is the world's largest producer contributing nearly 40% of global output — which Indian state currently holds the top position in mango production?

options.text: Maharashtra (famous for Alphonso/Hapus)
Uttar Pradesh (famous for Dashehari and Langra)
Andhra Pradesh (including divided Telangana region)
West Bengal (famous for Himsagar and Langra)

explanation: Uttar Pradesh is India's largest mango-producing state, contributing around 23–25% of national production. Key varieties grown include Dashehari, Langra, Chausa, and Fazli, with Malihabad near Lucknow being a famous mango belt with GI tag for Dashehari. Andhra Pradesh ranks second. Maharashtra leads in export-quality Alphonso but ranks fourth in total production. West Bengal is a significant producer but ranks lower than UP, AP, and Karnataka.

elimination_hints.hint: Maharashtra's Alphonso is famous for exports, not total production volume.
Malihabad (UP) is internationally renowned as a mango belt.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Commercial mango cultivation depends heavily on vegetative propagation to maintain varietal characteristics because mango is highly heterozygous and does not come true from seed — which propagation method is most widely used for mango in India?

options.text: Layering through air-layering technique using sphagnum moss ball
Softwood grafting (wedge/veneer) during June–August on one-year-old rootstocks
Seed sowing in polybags followed by direct planting in main field
Tissue culture micro-propagation on MS medium in commercial laboratories

explanation: Softwood grafting (also called wedge or epicotyl grafting) performed during June–August when rootstocks are in active growth is the most widely adopted mango propagation method in India, giving 80–90% success rate. Veneer grafting is popular too. Air-layering is used for some tropical fruits but not commercial mango. Mango is highly heterozygous, so seed propagation gives variable progeny. Tissue culture exists but is not mainstream for mango due to cost and lengthy protocols.

elimination_hints.hint: Seed propagation defeats the purpose — seedlings won't match the parent variety.
Tissue culture remains research-scale for mango; commercial nurseries depend on grafting.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Banana (Musa spp.) is India's most consumed fruit and the country ranks first globally in production — which of the following banana varieties belongs to the Cavendish group and dominates commercial banana cultivation in India?

options.text: Nendran (plantain type used for cooking and ripe table purpose)
Grand Naine (high-yielding tissue culture variety widely grown)
Red Banana (red-skinned variety with distinct flavour)
Poovan (tolerant to Panama wilt, popular in South India)

explanation: Grand Naine is a Cavendish (AAA genome) banana variety that has revolutionised commercial banana production in India since the 1990s, especially through tissue culture planting material. It yields 40–60 tonnes/ha, is suitable for export, and has uniform bunches. Dwarf Cavendish and Robusta are also Cavendish group varieties. Nendran is a plantain (AAB); Red Banana is a unique coloured variety; Poovan is another AAB type popular in Tamil Nadu. Tissue culture Grand Naine is the commercial standard.

elimination_hints.hint: Nendran is famous as a plantain for cooking, not a Cavendish dessert banana.
Red Banana's unique red peel places it outside the common yellow Cavendish group.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Panama wilt caused by Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense is the most devastating disease of banana worldwide, with Tropical Race 4 (TR4) now threatening global Cavendish production — which historical Cavendish-predecessor banana variety was wiped out by Race 1 of this pathogen?

options.text: Dwarf Cavendish (still widely cultivated today)
Gros Michel (commercially extinct from global trade by the 1960s)
Grand Naine (released to replace Gros Michel)
Nendran (South Indian plantain variety)

explanation: Gros Michel ('Big Mike') was the dominant global export banana until the 1960s, when Race 1 of Panama wilt (Fusarium oxysporum f. sp. cubense) devastated plantations worldwide, making it commercially extinct. Cavendish varieties (Dwarf Cavendish, Grand Naine) resistant to Race 1 replaced Gros Michel. Now Tropical Race 4 (TR4), first reported in India from Bihar in 2018, threatens Cavendish itself, signalling another potential banana crisis. This history makes banana a classic case of genetic vulnerability in monoculture.

elimination_hints.hint: Cavendish varieties were bred/selected specifically as the replacement — they weren't the victim.
Nendran is a plantain variety, not a global export banana.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Citrus fruits form a major commercial group in Indian horticulture with distinct varietal groups for different uses — which citrus fruit is commonly called the Nagpur mandarin and enjoys a Geographical Indication (GI) tag for its region of origin?

options.text: Citrus sinensis (sweet orange / mosambi)
Citrus reticulata (loose-skinned mandarin orange)
Citrus aurantifolia (Kagzi lime / acid lime)
Citrus paradisi (grapefruit)

explanation: The Nagpur mandarin is a variety of Citrus reticulata (mandarin orange), famously cultivated in the Vidarbha region of Maharashtra around Nagpur. It is characterised by loose skin (easy peeling), sweet pulp, and orange-yellow colour. It holds a GI tag and is exported widely. Maharashtra's Nagpur region is known as the 'Orange City'. C. sinensis is the sweet orange (mosambi), C. aurantifolia is acid lime, and C. paradisi is grapefruit — none of which are called Nagpur mandarin.

elimination_hints.hint: The term 'mandarin' directly matches Citrus reticulata — direct taxonomic clue.
Mosambi (sweet orange) is a different citrus — not called mandarin.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Citrus greening disease, also called Huanglongbing (HLB), is regarded as the most destructive citrus disease globally — what is the primary insect vector responsible for its spread in citrus orchards?

options.text: Citrus leafminer (Phyllocnistis citrella) tunnelling young leaves
Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri) transmitting the Candidatus Liberibacter bacterium
Citrus blackfly (Aleurocanthus woglumi) sucking sap from undersides
Citrus mealybug (Planococcus citri) infesting stems and fruits

explanation: The Asian citrus psyllid (Diaphorina citri) is the principal vector of Candidatus Liberibacter asiaticus, the bacterium that causes citrus greening or Huanglongbing (HLB). The psyllid transmits the bacterium when feeding on phloem sap. Infected trees show yellow shoots, mottled leaves, misshapen bitter fruits, and eventually die. HLB has destroyed citrus industries in Florida, Brazil, and parts of Asia. Other listed insects cause damage but do not vector this specific disease.

elimination_hints.hint: C and D are sucking pests but not known HLB vectors — specificity matters.
The leafminer causes leaf damage but doesn't transmit the HLB pathogen.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Guava (Psidium guajava) is considered the 'apple of the tropics' and the 'poor man's apple' due to its affordability and high nutritional value — which cultivar is widely recognised as the most popular commercial guava variety in India?

options.text: Red Lady (a papaya variety)
Allahabad Safeda (white pulp, round fruit, high TSS)
Pusa Delicious (a papaya cultivar from IARI)
Bangalore Blue (a grape variety)

explanation: Allahabad Safeda is India's most famous and widely cultivated guava variety. It produces round white-pulped fruits with high TSS (12–13°Brix), fewer seeds, and excellent keeping quality. The Allahabad (now Prayagraj) region in UP is historically the heart of guava cultivation in India. Lucknow-49 (Sardar) is another popular variety derived from Allahabad Safeda. Red Lady is a papaya variety, Pusa Delicious is also papaya, and Bangalore Blue is a grape cultivar.

elimination_hints.hint: Red Lady is a widely-grown papaya hybrid — not a guava.
Bangalore Blue is a distinct grape variety — not guava.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Papaya (Carica papaya) is a valuable commercial fruit that also yields papain, an important proteolytic enzyme with industrial applications — from which part of the papaya plant is papain commercially extracted?

options.text: Ripe fruit pulp after full maturation and colour development
Unripe green fruit by making shallow incisions on the surface to collect latex
Leaves after drying and powdering for water extraction
Roots after harvesting the plant at the end of its productive life

explanation: Papain is extracted from the white milky latex of unripe (green) papaya fruits. Shallow incisions are made on the fruit surface in the morning hours; the exuded latex is collected, dried, and processed into powder or flakes. Papain is a proteolytic enzyme used as a meat tenderiser, in leather tanning, in pharmaceuticals (digestive aids), and in textile industry. Ripe fruit has negligible latex. Leaves and roots are not commercial sources.

elimination_hints.hint: Ripe fruits lack significant latex — the enzyme is associated with the unripe milky sap.
Papain is an enzyme from latex, which is produced in the fruit, not leaves or roots for commercial scale.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Grape (Vitis vinifera) cultivation in India is concentrated in specific states due to its temperate to sub-tropical requirements — which Indian state accounts for the highest grape production contributing over 80% of national output?

options.text: Karnataka
Tamil Nadu
Maharashtra (especially Nashik and Sangli districts)
Andhra Pradesh

explanation: Maharashtra overwhelmingly dominates Indian grape production, contributing over 80% of the national output. The Nashik district is the undisputed grape capital of India, accounting for nearly half of all Indian grapes. Sangli and Solapur are other major districts. Maharashtra's semi-arid climate with hot days and cool nights during ripening favours grape quality. Karnataka (Bijapur) and Tamil Nadu are secondary producers. Thompson Seedless dominates commercial plantings, with Nashik also being home to India's wine industry.

elimination_hints.hint: Nashik is globally recognised as India's 'Wine Capital' — direct clue to Maharashtra.
Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh are too tropical/humid for significant grape cultivation.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Apple (Malus domestica) is a temperate fruit that requires specific chilling hours to break dormancy and produce fruits, limiting its commercial cultivation to specific regions in India — which state is the largest producer of apples?

options.text: Himachal Pradesh
Jammu & Kashmir (including Ladakh)
Uttarakhand
Arunachal Pradesh

explanation: Jammu & Kashmir is India's largest apple-producing state, contributing around 70–75% of national apple production. The Kashmir Valley's ideal temperate climate, sufficient chilling hours (800–1200), and suitable altitudes make it the apple bowl of India. Himachal Pradesh ranks second with 15–20% share (Shimla, Kullu). Uttarakhand and Arunachal Pradesh are minor producers. Popular varieties include Red Delicious, Golden Delicious, Ambri, Royal Delicious, and the newer Gala and Fuji.

elimination_hints.hint: Kashmir apples are globally associated with the state — J&K is the dominant producer.
Arunachal Pradesh has very limited apple area despite being a north-eastern hill state.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each fruit crop with the most popular commercial cultivar grown in India along with its key distinguishing characteristic used by farmers for variety identification.
column_b: Thompson Seedless — white seedless berry preferred for table use and raisin making
Alphonso (Hapus) — saffron-yellow pulp with distinct aroma, premium export variety from Ratnagiri
Allahabad Safeda — round fruit with white crispy pulp and high TSS of 12–13 °Brix
Grand Naine — Cavendish group tall plant with uniform bunches for commercial export

correct_mapping.Mango: Alphonso (Hapus) — saffron-yellow pulp with distinct aroma, premium export variety from Ratnagiri
correct_mapping.Banana: Grand Naine — Cavendish group tall plant with uniform bunches for commercial export
correct_mapping.Guava: Allahabad Safeda — round fruit with white crispy pulp and high TSS of 12–13 °Brix
correct_mapping.Grape: Thompson Seedless — white seedless berry preferred for table use and raisin making

options.text: Mango→Alphonso; Banana→Grand Naine; Guava→Allahabad Safeda; Grape→Thompson Seedless
Mango→Thompson Seedless; Banana→Alphonso; Guava→Grand Naine; Grape→Allahabad Safeda
Mango→Grand Naine; Banana→Thompson Seedless; Guava→Alphonso; Grape→Allahabad Safeda
Mango→Allahabad Safeda; Banana→Alphonso; Guava→Thompson Seedless; Grape→Grand Naine

explanation: Alphonso (Hapus) from Ratnagiri, Maharashtra, is the world's most prized export mango with saffron pulp and GI tag. Grand Naine is the dominant Cavendish commercial banana in India and globally. Allahabad Safeda is the iconic Indian guava with white crispy pulp. Thompson Seedless is the most widely grown commercial grape in India, suitable for both table and raisin (kismis) production. Each variety is a flagship cultivar for its fruit crop.

elimination_hints.hint: Alphonso is synonymous with mango export — impossible to place elsewhere.
Grand Naine's Cavendish identity places it firmly with banana.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each fruit crop with its commercially recommended propagation method used in Indian nurseries for producing true-to-type planting material at scale.
column_b: Hardwood cuttings taken during dormant season and treated with IBA for rapid rooting
Softwood or veneer grafting on one-year-old seedling rootstocks during active growth period
Sword suckers from mother plant or tissue culture plantlets for uniform commercial plantings
Seed propagation using fresh seeds from selected gynodioecious parents in disease-free nurseries

correct_mapping.Mango: Softwood or veneer grafting on one-year-old seedling rootstocks during active growth period
correct_mapping.Banana: Sword suckers from mother plant or tissue culture plantlets for uniform commercial plantings
correct_mapping.Grape: Hardwood cuttings taken during dormant season and treated with IBA for rapid rooting
correct_mapping.Papaya: Seed propagation using fresh seeds from selected gynodioecious parents in disease-free nurseries

options.text: Mango→Softwood grafting; Banana→Sword suckers/TC; Grape→Hardwood cuttings; Papaya→Seed propagation
Mango→Seed propagation; Banana→Hardwood cuttings; Grape→Softwood grafting; Papaya→Sword suckers/TC
Mango→Hardwood cuttings; Banana→Seed propagation; Grape→Sword suckers/TC; Papaya→Softwood grafting
Mango→Sword suckers/TC; Banana→Softwood grafting; Grape→Seed propagation; Papaya→Hardwood cuttings

explanation: Mango is heterozygous and propagated by softwood/veneer grafting for variety maintenance. Banana, being seedless, is propagated via sword suckers or tissue culture — Grand Naine TC plantlets dominate commercial plantations. Grape is typically propagated through hardwood cuttings of 25–30 cm lengths taken during dormant pruning season, treated with IBA. Papaya is seed-propagated due to its short perennial life (3–4 years); farmers use fresh seeds from superior gynodioecious selections.

elimination_hints.hint: Banana lacks viable seeds — rules out seed propagation; suckers/TC are the options.
Mango seedlings don't retain varietal traits — requires grafting.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Match each Indian state with its primary fruit crop for which it is the top national producer accounting for a significant share of India's production.
column_b: Apple — nearly 75% of national production with Kashmir Valley as core zone
Papaya — India's largest producer with Krishna and Guntur districts leading
Banana — top producer across Cauvery delta and western districts
Mango — nearly 25% share led by Dashehari and Langra varieties from Malihabad belt

correct_mapping.Uttar Pradesh: Mango — nearly 25% share led by Dashehari and Langra varieties from Malihabad belt
correct_mapping.Tamil Nadu: Banana — top producer across Cauvery delta and western districts
correct_mapping.Andhra Pradesh: Papaya — India's largest producer with Krishna and Guntur districts leading
correct_mapping.Jammu & Kashmir: Apple — nearly 75% of national production with Kashmir Valley as core zone

options.text: UP→Mango; TN→Banana; AP→Papaya; J&K→Apple
UP→Apple; TN→Mango; AP→Banana; J&K→Papaya
UP→Banana; TN→Apple; AP→Mango; J&K→Papaya
UP→Papaya; TN→Apple; AP→Banana; J&K→Mango

explanation: UP leads in mango (25% share — Dashehari/Langra belt of Malihabad). Tamil Nadu is India's top banana producer with vast plantations across the Cauvery delta (Tiruchirappalli, Thanjavur) and western districts (Theni, Coimbatore). Andhra Pradesh leads in papaya production (Krishna, Guntur being key districts). J&K dominates apple with 70–75% share. These state-crop associations are textbook exam facts.

elimination_hints.hint: Apple is impossible to associate with warm states like UP, TN, or AP — temperate only.
The Kashmir Valley and apple association is globally known.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Analyse the Assertion and Reason below about mango flowering biology and choose the option that best describes the relationship between the two statements.
assertion: Mango tree shows strong biennial bearing tendency where a heavy crop year (on-year) is typically followed by a poor crop year (off-year) in many cultivars like Dashehari and Langra.
reason: Heavy fruiting depletes carbohydrate reserves and hormone balance in the tree, reducing flower bud differentiation during the next season's flowering window and causing alternate bearing behaviour.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — biennial or alternate bearing is a well-documented trait in many Indian mango cultivars (Dashehari, Langra, Chausa), though some like Totapuri and Bangalora are regular bearers. The reason correctly explains the physiological basis: heavy fruiting in the 'on' year depletes stored carbohydrates and alters the gibberellin/cytokinin balance that controls flower bud initiation, suppressing flowering the following year. Practices like deblossoming, proper canopy management, and paclobutrazol application help mitigate this.

elimination_hints.hint: Alternate/biennial bearing in mango is a classical horticultural phenomenon.
The carbohydrate-hormone depletion explanation is the standard scientific basis.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate the following Assertion and Reason about tissue culture banana plants and determine the correctness of each and their relationship.
assertion: Tissue culture-raised banana plants have become the preferred planting material for commercial banana cultivation in India over traditional sucker-based propagation.
reason: Tissue culture plants are disease-free (especially Panama wilt and bunchy top virus), genetically uniform, yield earlier with synchronised bunching, and provide higher productivity in commercial plantations.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — tissue culture banana plantlets (Grand Naine being the flagship) now dominate commercial banana cultivation in India. The reason correctly explains the advantages: TC plants are virus-indexed and disease-free, produce genetically uniform crops enabling synchronised harvest (critical for export), reduce crop cycle by 2–3 months, and yield 30–50 tonnes/ha compared to 20–30 tonnes for sucker-based plantings. NHM and state governments subsidise TC plantlets, reinforcing adoption.

elimination_hints.hint: Tissue culture's dominance in Indian banana is a widely documented fact.
Disease-free + uniform + higher yield — exactly the advantages taught in every textbook.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Read the Assertion and Reason about India's global position in fruit production and determine whether both are correct and if the reason explains the assertion appropriately.
assertion: India is the second-largest producer of fruits in the world after China and ranks first globally in the production of banana, mango, and papaya.
reason: India's diverse agro-climatic zones ranging from temperate hills to tropical coastlines enable cultivation of a wide variety of tropical, sub-tropical, and temperate fruits across different states.

options.text: Both Assertion and Reason are correct, and Reason is the correct explanation of Assertion
Both Assertion and Reason are correct, but Reason is NOT the correct explanation of Assertion
Assertion is correct but Reason is incorrect
Assertion is incorrect

explanation: The assertion is correct — India is the world's second-largest fruit producer after China and ranks first in banana, mango, papaya, and pomegranate production globally. The reason correctly explains the basis: India's 15 agro-climatic zones support an extraordinary range of fruit crops — apples and pears in Kashmir, grapes in Maharashtra, mango across plains, coconut/banana in coastal south. This climatic diversity is the fundamental reason for India's fruit production leadership. The reason directly justifies the assertion.

elimination_hints.hint: India's #1 status in banana, mango, papaya is a well-known statistic.
The agro-climatic diversity argument is the standard explanation in horticulture textbooks.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following operations in the correct chronological sequence for establishing a new commercial mango orchard from land preparation to first economic harvest in Indian conditions.
items: Layout and pit digging at recommended spacing of 10×10 m, followed by pit filling with manure and topsoil
Planting grafted mango saplings at the onset of monsoon with adequate staking and watering
Training and pruning during juvenile phase to develop strong scaffold framework and canopy
First economic harvest typically from 5th to 6th year onwards with full bearing by 10th–12th year

options.text: Layout and pit digging → Planting saplings → Training and pruning → First economic harvest
Planting saplings → Layout and pit digging → First economic harvest → Training and pruning
Training and pruning → First economic harvest → Layout and pit digging → Planting saplings
First economic harvest → Training and pruning → Planting saplings → Layout and pit digging

explanation: New mango orchard establishment follows a defined sequence: (1) layout the orchard with 10×10 m spacing using square/quincunx system and dig pits of 1×1×1 m, fill with FYM and topsoil at least a month before planting; (2) plant grafted mango saplings at the onset of monsoon for good establishment; (3) during the 3–4 year juvenile phase, train the plant to develop a strong scaffold and prune unwanted branches; (4) mango begins economic bearing from 5th–6th year and reaches full yield by 10th–12th year.

elimination_hints.hint: Harvest is always the final outcome — cannot come first.
Pit digging must precede planting — saplings cannot be planted without prepared pits.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Arrange the following stages of banana plant development from planting to harvest in the correct chronological order as observed in commercial tissue culture banana plantations.
items: Planting of hardened tissue culture plantlet at 1.8×1.8 m spacing in a well-prepared pit
Active vegetative growth with production of pseudostem, leaves, and suckers over 6–8 months
Flowering with emergence of inflorescence (bunch) from the pseudostem at 8–10 months
Harvesting of mature bunches at full three-fourths fruit development around 11–14 months

options.text: Planting of plantlet → Vegetative growth → Flowering → Harvesting
Flowering → Planting → Harvesting → Vegetative growth
Vegetative growth → Harvesting → Planting → Flowering
Harvesting → Flowering → Vegetative growth → Planting

explanation: Banana life cycle in commercial plantations: (1) plant hardened TC plantlet (1.8×1.8 m spacing gives ~3000 plants/ha); (2) 6–8 months of vegetative growth with pseudostem formation, active leaf production (30–40 leaves), and sucker emergence; (3) flowering occurs at 8–10 months when the inflorescence emerges from the pseudostem; (4) bunches are harvested at 11–14 months when fruits reach three-fourths maturity for shipping or full maturity for local markets. Total crop cycle is about 12–14 months.

elimination_hints.hint: Planting is always the first operation — nothing can precede it.
Harvesting is the final economic outcome — closes the cycle.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Evaluate whether the following statement is true or false: India is the world's largest producer of banana, mango, and papaya, and also ranks first globally in the production of pomegranate, making horticulture a major contributor to Indian agriculture.

options.text: True
False

explanation: This statement is true. India holds the world's #1 rank in production of banana (contributing ~25% of global output), mango (~40%), papaya (~35%), and pomegranate. India also ranks second globally in total fruit production after China. The horticulture sector now contributes over 30% of the agricultural GVA despite using less than 20% of cultivated land — a testament to its high value per unit area. Maharashtra leads in pomegranate with Solapur district being the core belt.

elimination_hints.hint: India's leadership in banana, mango, papaya is consistently reported in FAO statistics.
Pomegranate's Solapur–Maharashtra belt is widely known in horticultural literature.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture

question_text: Study the diagram showing major fruit crops of India along with their leading producing states, famous varieties, and propagation methods, then identify the correct crop-state-variety-propagation combination.

options.text: Mango — Uttar Pradesh — Dashehari — softwood grafting on seedling rootstock
Banana — Jammu & Kashmir — Grand Naine — hardwood cuttings in dormant season
Apple — Andhra Pradesh — Red Delicious — sucker propagation from mother plant
Grape — Uttar Pradesh — Alphonso — seed propagation in polybags

explanation: From the diagram, mango is correctly identified with UP (top producer), Dashehari (famous variety from Malihabad), and softwood grafting (standard propagation method). Banana's leading state is Tamil Nadu (not J&K) and it is propagated via suckers/TC (not hardwood cuttings). Apple is grown in J&K (not AP, which is too warm) and propagated by budding/grafting (not suckers). Grape is grown in Maharashtra (not UP), with Thompson Seedless (Alphonso is a mango, not a grape), and propagated through hardwood cuttings.

elimination_hints.hint: Alphonso is always mango — never grape; eliminates D immediately.
Apple cannot be grown in AP — too warm; J&K is the apple state.

topic: Fruit Production: Mango, Papaya, Banana, Guava, Citrus and Grapes
subject: Agriculture
